title: Isaiah 50
subtitle: The Third Servant Song: I Offered My Back to Those Who Beat Me

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “Where Is Your Mother’s Certificate of Divorce?”

heb.paragraph: "Where is your mother’s certificate of divorce (kərîtūṭ) with which I sent her away? To which of my creditors did I sell you?" (v.1). God denies divorce and debt-sale: Israel was not divorced or sold; they were sent away for their own sins (v.1). "Was my arm too short to deliver you? Do I lack the strength to rescue you?" (v.2). The arm that dried up the sea and turned rivers to desert is not shortened (vv.2–3). The exile is not abandonment; it’s discipline within an unbroken marriage.

notes.note: "Where is the certificate of divorce?" God demands the legal document. There is none. The marriage covenant stands. Exile is separation, not divorce.
"Was my arm too short to deliver? I dry up the sea; I clothe the heavens with darkness." The arm-of-the-LORD theme: the power that parted the Red Sea has not weakened.

refs.note: The divorce-certificate question evokes Mosaic legislation: if YHWH divorced Israel, where is the document?
Jeremiah says YHWH gave faithless Israel a certificate of divorce — Isaiah's question implies the answer is 'no such certificate exists.'
Hosea's marriage metaphor — she is not my wife, I am not her husband — parallels the divorce language, yet promises restoration.
YHWH calls Israel back like a wife forsaken and grieved in spirit — the divorce question answered with remarriage.

hist.context: The chapter opens with rhetorical questions: where is your mother's divorce certificate? To which creditors was I forced to sell you (v.1)? The implied answer: none. Israel's exile is discipline, not divorce. YHWH's arm is not too short to save (v.2). The passage reframes exile: Israel was sold for her sins, not because YHWH was bankrupt. This legal-metaphor section sets up the Third Servant Song by clearing away the notion that YHWH abandoned his people permanently.

sections.header: Verses 4–11 — “I Offered My Back to Those Who Beat Me”

heb.paragraph: "The Sovereign LORD has given me a well-instructed (limmūdîm) tongue, to know the word that sustains the weary" (v.4). THE THIRD SERVANT SONG (50:4–9). The servant is a learner: "He wakens me morning by morning, wakens my ear to listen like one being instructed" (v.4). Then the cost: "I offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard; I did not hide my face from mocking and spitting" (v.6). The Passion narrative before the Passion: Jesus’ flogging, beard-pulling, spitting (Matt 26:67; 27:26, 30). "Because the Sovereign LORD helps me, I will not be disgraced. I have set my face like flint" (v.7). Luke 9:51: "Jesus resolutely set his face to go to Jerusalem" — echoing this verse.

notes.note: "He wakens me morning by morning, wakens my ear to listen." The servant’s daily discipline: morning listening. The tongue that sustains the weary comes from the ear that listens first.

"I offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard; I did not hide my face from mocking and spitting." The Passion foretold: beating, beard-pulling, spitting. Matt 26:67; 27:26, 30 fulfil this verse-by-verse.

"I have set my face like flint. It is the Sovereign LORD who helps me. Who will condemn me?" Luke 9:51: Jesus "set his face" to go to Jerusalem. Romans 8:33–34: "Who will bring any charge? Who is the one who condemns?" — Paul’s questions echo the servant’s confidence.

"Let the one who walks in the dark and has no light trust in the name of the LORD. But you who light fires and provide yourselves with flaming torches — walk in the light of your fires! This is what you shall receive from my hand: you will lie down in torment." Two paths: trust God’s unseen light or light your own fire. Self-illumination leads to torment.

refs.note: The spitting and striking during Jesus's trial echo the Servant's suffering — beatings, pulled beard, spit on face.
The soldiers spit on Jesus and struck his head — the passion narrative shaped by Isaiah's Servant portrait.
Peter describes Christ as one who did not retaliate when insulted, entrusting himself to the righteous judge — direct allusion to Isaiah 50.
Jesus endured the cross, despising its shame, for the joy set before him — the Servant's resolve mirrored.

hist.context: The Third Servant Song (50:4–9) introduces suffering explicitly. The Servant is YHWH's disciple (limmûd), awakened morning by morning to hear and teach. He offers his back to those who beat him, his cheeks to those who pull out his beard, his face to mocking and spitting. Yet he sets his face like flint, confident that YHWH will vindicate him (vv.7–9). This is not passive victimhood but resolute trust. The legal metaphor returns: 'Who will bring charges against me?' The Servant expects acquittal from the heavenly court even as he endures earthly abuse.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Sovereign Lord has given me a well-instructed tongue, to know the word that sustains the weary. He wakens me morning by morning, wakens my ear to listen like one being instructed.ESVThe Lord GOD has given me the tongue of those who are taught, that I may know how to sustain with a word him who is weary. Morning by morning he awakens; he awakens my ear to hear as those who are taught.NIVBecause the Sovereign Lord helps me, I will not be disgraced. Therefore have I set my face like flint, and I know I will not be put to shame.ESVBut the Lord GOD helps me; therefore I have not been disgraced; therefore I have set my face like a flint, and I know that I shall not be put to shame.

rec.text: ""Where is the certificate of divorce?" God demands the legal document. There is none. The marriage covenant stands. Exile is separation, not divorce."

rows.label: Not Divorced — 50:1–3
rows.text: "Where is the CERTIFICATE OF DIVORCE?" There is none. God’s arm NOT too short. Marriage stands.

hebtext.note: "Where is your mother’s certificate of divorce (kərîtūṭ) with which I sent her away? To which of my creditors did I sell you?" (v.1). God denies divorce and debt-sale: Israel was not divorced or sold; they were sent away for their own sins (v.1). "Was my arm too short to deliver you? Do I lack the strength to rescue you?" (v.2). The arm that dried up the sea and turned rivers to desert is not shortened (vv.2–3). The exile is not abandonment; it’s discipline within an unbroken marriage.

"The Sovereign LORD has given me a well-instructed (limmūdîm) tongue, to know the word that sustains the weary" (v.4). THE THIRD SERVANT SONG (50:4–9). The servant is a learner: "He wakens me morning by morning, wakens my ear to listen like one being instructed" (v.4). Then the cost: "I offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard; I did not hide my face from mocking and spitting" (v.6). The Passion narrative before the Passion: Jesus’ flogging, beard-pulling, spitting (Matt 26:67; 27:26, 30). "Because the Sovereign LORD helps me, I will not be disgraced. I have set my face like flint" (v.7). Luke 9:51: "Jesus resolutely set his face to go to Jerusalem" — echoing this verse.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the third Servant Song. Beating, spitting, beard-pulling (Matt 26–27). Face like flint (Luke 9:51). "Who will condemn?" (Rom 8:33). Faith at 9: trust in darkness.

coaching.tip: 'Where is your mother's certificate of divorce?' (v. 1). God uses legal language to deny that the covenant is terminated. Israel in exile feels abandoned, divorced. God's response is judicial: produce the documents. There are none. Exile isn't divorce — it's discipline. The distinction matters immensely: discipline presumes ongoing relationship; divorce ends it.

The third Servant Song (vv. 4–9) describes a figure who 'offered my back to those who beat me, my cheeks to those who pulled out my beard' (v. 6). The Servant doesn't resist or retaliate. The Gospels apply this directly to Jesus's trial and crucifixion. Isaiah's Servant accepts suffering not as defeat but as vocation. The most powerful person in the room is the one who chooses not to fight back.

prayer_prompt: As the Servant says 'I offered my back to those who beat me' and 'the Sovereign LORD helps me,' consider asking God for the courage to face a costly obedience without retreating into self-protection.
